description:
You have just inherited a legacy Node-based dashboard project that lives in
    /home/user/project.  
    Yesterday the web server mis-behaved and the operations team dropped two raw
    Nginx access logs in the sub-folder /home/user/project/logs:

        • 2023-08-14_access.log  
        • 2023-08-15_access.log   ← this is the file we care about

    Your job is to produce three different REGEX-driven extracts **and** a tiny
    verification log for 15 August 2023.

    1) 5×× request list  
       • Path: /home/user/project/reports/5xx_2023-08-15.log  
       • Must contain **exactly** every line from 2023-08-15_access.log whose
         HTTP status code matches the regular expression 5\d\d (i.e. 500-599).  
       • Keep the lines unchanged and in their original order.  
       • No extra blank lines are allowed.

    2) Daily status-code summary  
       • Path: /home/user/project/reports/daily_summary_2023-08-15.csv  
       • Each line must be exactly `<STATUS_CODE>,<COUNT>` (no spaces).  
       • Include every distinct status code that appears in
         2023-08-15_access.log.  
       • Sort ascending by the numeric status code.  
       • Do NOT add a header row.

    3) Curl authentication errors  
       • Path: /home/user/project/reports/curl_auth_errors_2023-08-15.log  
       • Copy, unmodified and in original order, all lines whose status is
         401 **or** 403 AND whose User-Agent contains the literal text “curl”.  
       • No extra blank lines.

    4) Verification log  
       • Path: /home/user/project/reports/regex_filter.log  
       • Exactly three lines, one per file created above, in the precise order
         shown below.  
       • Format: `<FILENAME>: <LINE_COUNT>`  
         (Note: only the base file name, *not* the full path.)

             5xx_2023-08-15.log: <count>
             daily_summary_2023-08-15.csv: <count>
             curl_auth_errors_2023-08-15.log: <count>

       The automated grader will compare the counts you write here with the
       actual number of lines that appear in each file.

    Additional notes
    • Create the /home/user/project/reports directory yourself if it doesn’t
      exist.  You do **not** need sudo or any special permissions to do so.  
    • Do all work from the command line; feel free to inspect files, test
      regular expressions, etc.  
    • The only files the grader will read are the four paths listed above, so
      don’t worry about cleaning up any temporary artifacts.
